strategy: Preserve the transcript anchor and directly verify the missing visual facts: which hand is the left-side player after Wild Shuffle Hands, and how many cards that hand ends up with in the post-redistribution sequence.

steps.purpose: Use the anchored transcript plus the retrieved post-anchor frame sequence to identify table orientation, determine which visible hand corresponds to the player on the left of the Wild Shuffle Hands player, and count that player's final redistributed cards from the local sequence.

inputs.query: Using the transcript rule explanation and the chronological frames after it, determine: (1) who is the 'player to your left' relative to the player who played Wild Shuffle Hands, based on table orientation and dealing order visible in the sequence; (2) which visible hand in these frames corresponds to that left-side player; (3) how many cards that player has after the redistribution in the shown sequence; and (4) which answer option A-E matches that count. Do not rely on prior unsupported claims; verify from the frames and transcript together.
text_contexts: Audit gaps to resolve: which visible hand corresponds to the player on the left after Wild Shuffle Hands; the final number of cards received by that player; map that grounded count to one answer option.
Known anchor: the Wild Shuffle Hands explanation is at 119.827s-143.333s, and the retrieved visual sequence is later at 148s-152s.

segments.text: Wild Shuffle Hands. This card is a wild and can be played on anything. Once played, collect every card from all the players' hands and shuffle them together. Starting with the player to your left, deal out all the cards to all the players. Players may end up with more or fewer cards than what they started with. Play continues in the current direction of play with you picking the color.

Wild draw four. This card is a wild and can be played on anything. However, you are only allowed to play this card if you do not have any other cards in your hand with the same color as the top card in the discard. Once played, the next player may either draw four cards and lose their turn,

or they may challenge. If you challenge a wild draw four, then the player who played it must reveal their hand. If they do not have the color in their hand that matches the discard pile they played to, then you must draw six cards and lose your turn. If they do have the color, then they must draw four cards instead, and it is your turn. Regardless of the outcome of the challenge, that player picks the color that continues play.

expected_outputs.answer: grounded count for the left-side player and mapped option
expected_outputs.supporting_points: explicit orientation, hand identification, and count evidence from the frame sequence
expected_outputs.analysis: whether the sequence truly shows the final redistributed hand count or remains ambiguous

refinement_instructions: Preserve the existing transcript anchor and the fact that earlier analysis found 4 visible cards but lacked referent grounding. In the trace, do not treat the prior 4-card observation as sufficient by itself. Instead, require the new synthesis to explicitly establish table orientation, identify which hand is the left-side player, and state why the 148s-152s sequence represents that player's post-redistribution count. If the tool still cannot ground left-player identity or finality from these frames, keep the answer unresolved rather than promoting the prior unsupported 4-card claim.
